cards.front: What are the 7 Terraform block types?
cards.back:
terraform {} — backend + required_providers
provider {} — cloud provider config
variable {} — input values
locals {} — computed/derived values
data {} — read existing infrastructure
resource {} — create/manage infrastructure
output {} — export values

resource creates and manages infrastructure — Terraform owns its lifecycle. data reads existing infrastructure Terraform doesn't manage.
VPC created by another team = data source

cards.front: How do you reference a local value?
cards.back:
local.name — singular, no 's'. locals.name is WRONG and will error.
Define in locals {} block, reference as local.name everywhere

Downloads provider plugins and upgrades to latest allowed version.
Always use -upgrade in CoderPad — avoids stale provider cache that causes init failures

terraform fmt
→ terraform init -upgrade
→ terraform validate
→ terraform plan
→ terraform apply.
fmt first shows discipline.
validate catches syntax in 1 second

Checks syntax and internal consistency without contacting any remote services.
Catches missing required arguments, wrong types, invalid references.
Runs in 1 second

Shows what will be created/changed/destroyed without making changes.
Outputs show in plan even without apply — use this to verify locals and conditionals

cards.front: How do you force recreation of a resource?
cards.back:
terraform apply -replace=aws_db_instance.main — replaces specific resource.
Old command was terraform taint (deprecated in 0.15)

Formats .tf files to canonical style — consistent indentation, alignment.
Like a linter.
Run before every commit and before CoderPad submission

count creates by index — removing middle item shifts all indexes, causes destroy/recreate. for_each creates by key — safe to remove any item.
Use for_each for maps and sets, count only for simple identical resources

JSON file mapping your config to real infrastructure.
Tracks resource IDs, metadata, dependencies.
Never commit to Git — contains sensitive data including passwords

backend "s3" {
  bucket       = "terraform-state"
  key          = "rds/terraform.tfstate"
  region       = "us-east-1"
  use_lockfile = true
  encrypt      = true
}
use_lockfile requires Terraform >= 1.10

Terraform >= 1.10 native S3 locking via bucket versioning — no DynamoDB table needed.
Prevents two engineers running apply simultaneously.
One gets a lock error

cards.front: What was the old state locking method?
cards.back:
DynamoDB table with LockID as partition key.
Still works but deprecated in favor of use_lockfile = true with S3 versioning.
Terraform 1.10+ makes DynamoDB unnecessary

Brings existing infrastructure under Terraform management without recreating it. terraform import aws_db_instance.main <rds-identifier>.
State is updated, config must be written manually

multi_az = var.environment != "dev" — bool shorthand.
Or: multi_az = var.environment == "prod" ? true : false.
First form is cleaner

db_identifier = "myapp-${var.environment}-postgres" — uses ${}.
Also: secret_name = "/${var.environment}/rds/password".
Produces: myapp-prod-postgres

cards.front: How to verify locals without apply?
cards.back:
Add output "config_summary" { value = { instance_class = local.db_instance_class, multi_az = local.multi_az } } then run terraform plan -var="environment=prod".
Outputs show in plan, no apply needed

cards.front: What locals does a DB engineer need per environment?
cards.back:
db_instance_class, multi_az, backup_retention, db_identifier, deletion_protection, skip_final_snapshot — all derived from var.environment.
No hardcoded values in resource block

skip_final_snapshot = var.environment == "dev" — true for dev (destroy freely), false for staging/prod.
If false, must also set final_snapshot_identifier or plan will error

AWS-managed relational database.
AWS handles OS patching, engine upgrades, backups, failover, hardware.
You handle schema, queries, parameter tuning, access control

PostgreSQL (port 5432), MySQL (3306), MariaDB (3306), Oracle (1521), SQL Server (1433), Db2.
Aurora is separate AWS-native product with same API

db.t3.* — burstable, dev/test, free tier (micro)
db.t4g.* — ARM Graviton, cheaper
db.m6g.* — general purpose production
db.r6g.* — memory optimized, large datasets

gp2 — old SSD, free tier eligible. gp3 — new SSD, cheaper and faster baseline, use in production. io1/io2 — provisioned IOPS for high-performance workloads.
Always gp3 in prod

Synchronous replication to standby in another AZ.
Automatic failover in 60-120 seconds.
Same endpoint — app reconnects automatically.
Standby is NOT readable.
Costs 2x

Asynchronous replication to readable copy.
Separate endpoint.
Up to 5 per primary.
Same region or cross-region.
Can be promoted to standalone.
Used for read offload and DR

cards.front: Multi-AZ vs Read Replica — key difference?
cards.back:
Multi-AZ = HA (synchronous, auto-failover, NOT readable, same endpoint).
Read Replica = performance/DR (async, readable, separate endpoint, manual failover via promote)

cards.front: Can you read from a Multi-AZ standby?
cards.back:
No.
The standby is for failover only — not readable.
To offload reads, add a Read Replica.
Multi-AZ standby becomes primary only during failover

db.t3.micro only.
20GB gp2 storage.
750 hours/month.
Single-AZ only (Multi-AZ not free).
One instance at a time.
Destroy same day to avoid charges (~$14/month if forgotten)

cards.front: Can you enable encryption after RDS creation?
cards.back:
No — encryption cannot be enabled on an existing unencrypted instance.
Must snapshot the instance, copy snapshot with encryption enabled, restore from encrypted snapshot

Customizes database engine settings. family must match engine version exactly (postgres16, mysql8.0).
Used for audit logging: log_connections=1, log_disconnections=1, log_min_duration_statement=1000

postgres16 → PostgreSQL 16.x. postgres15 → PostgreSQL 15.x. mysql8.0 → MySQL 8.0.x. oracle-ee-19 → Oracle EE 19.x.
Mismatch causes apply error

AWS generates password, stores in Secrets Manager, auto-rotates.
Password NEVER appears in tfstate or env vars.
Get ARN from: aws_db_instance.main.master_user_secret[0].secret_arn

Prevents terraform destroy on production database.
Must set to false first, then apply, then destroy.
At a bank, losing the database is a catastrophic event — always true in prod

Database is in private subnet only — no internet route.
Never set to true in production.
For practice/testing only.
Combined with security group restricting port 5432 to VPC CIDR

["postgresql", "upgrade"] — exports PostgreSQL logs and upgrade logs to CloudWatch.
MySQL: ["audit", "error", "general", "slowquery"].
Required for compliance audit trail at banks

backup_window: "03:00-04:00" UTC — when automated backups run. maintenance_window: "sun:04:00-sun:05:00" — when patches apply.
They must NOT overlap or AWS rejects the config

skip_final_snapshot = false requires final_snapshot_identifier to be set or plan errors. skip_final_snapshot = true for dev (destroy freely).
Production: always false with identifier set

cards.front: Same-region vs cross-region replica in Terraform?
cards.back:
Same-region: replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.main.identifier.
Cross-region: replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.main.arn + provider = aws.dr alias.
ARN required for cross-region

cards.front: Security group for RDS — best practice?
cards.back:
Restrict port 5432 to app tier SG only (SG chaining), not VPC CIDR.
Pattern: ALB SG → App SG → RDS SG.
Each layer only accepts from layer above.
Never 0.0.0.0/0 on database port

source_security_group_id = aws_security_group.app.id — RDS only accepts from app SG, not entire VPC CIDR.
Tighter than CIDR-based rules.
Production best practice at banks

iam_database_authentication_enabled = true — app uses IAM token instead of password.
Token generated by aws rds generate-db-auth-token, expires in 15 minutes.
No long-lived credentials

Action: rds-db:connect.
Resource: arn:aws:rds-db:{region}:{account}:dbuser:{resource_id}/{username}.
Attach to EKS node role or IRSA role.
App never stores a password

IAM Roles for Service Accounts — EKS pods assume IAM roles without storing credentials.
Pod SA annotated with IAM role ARN.
OIDC federation exchanges projected token for STS credentials.
No access keys

cards.front: storage_encrypted = true — what does it encrypt?
cards.back:
Encrypts storage, automated backups, snapshots, and read replicas — all use AES-256 via KMS.
Cannot be enabled after creation.
In-transit: use sslmode=require in connection string

cards.front: How to handle RDS secrets in ECS?
cards.back:
Use secrets block in task definition referencing Secrets Manager ARN — never environment variables.
ECS agent fetches secret at task start.
Rotation is automatic with manage_master_user_password

Virtual Private Cloud — isolated network in AWS.
Contains subnets, route tables, internet gateway, NAT gateway, security groups, NACLs.
RDS must be deployed inside a VPC

Public subnet: route table has 0.0.0.0/0 → Internet Gateway.
Private subnet: route table has 0.0.0.0/0 → NAT Gateway.
RDS always in private subnet — no direct internet route

Security groups: stateful, instance-level, allow rules only.
NACLs: stateless, subnet-level, allow and deny rules.
SGs are the primary tool for RDS access control

data "aws_vpc" "main" { id = var.vpc_id } — reads existing VPC.
Access CIDR with data.aws_vpc.main.cidr_block.
Use in SG ingress to restrict RDS to VPC CIDR only

AWS-native regional router connecting VPCs, VPNs, Direct Connect without EC2 routing.
Replaces Transit VPC (old EC2-based hub).
Scales to thousands of VPCs.
Used at Fed to connect AWS to on-prem

Transit VPC: old pattern, EC2-based VPN appliances, complex, expensive, single point of failure.
Transit Gateway: AWS-native, no EC2, scales to 1000s of VPCs, route tables for segmentation

Dedicated private network connection from on-prem to AWS — not over internet.
Consistent bandwidth, lower latency.
Used at Fed for DMS migration traffic, DB replication, ongoing hybrid connectivity

Direct network connection between two VPCs — traffic stays on AWS backbone.
Not transitive (A↔B, B↔C does not mean A↔C).
Used for MongoDB Atlas and cross-account RDS access

Private endpoint for AWS services or third-party services — traffic never leaves AWS network.
Used for MongoDB Atlas (preferred over VPC peering at banks).
Also for RDS Proxy, Secrets Manager

Database Migration Service — migrates databases to AWS.
Supports Oracle, SQL Server, MySQL, PostgreSQL as source.
Three phases: schema conversion, full load, CDC (ongoing replication)

1.
Schema conversion: AWS SCT converts DDL (Oracle → PostgreSQL).
2.
Full load: DMS copies all existing data to RDS target.
3.
CDC: DMS streams ongoing changes via LogMiner until cutover

Schema Conversion Tool — converts Oracle/SQL Server DDL to PostgreSQL/MySQL syntax.
Flags incompatible objects (stored procedures, functions) that need manual rewrite.
Run before DMS

Change Data Capture — streams ongoing database changes after full load completes.
Uses Oracle LogMiner or SQL Server transaction log.
Keeps target in sync until cutover maintenance window

Completely different resources. aws_dms_replication_instance is the DMS engine that runs migration tasks. aws_db_instance is the RDS target database.
Both needed for migration infrastructure

cards.front: IAM role for DMS — what service?
cards.back:
Principal Service = dms.amazonaws.com — NOT ec2.amazonaws.com.
Common mistake under pressure.
DMS role needs SecretsManagerReadWrite to access source/target credentials

Separate from aws_db_subnet_group — DMS needs its own subnet group.
Specifies which subnets the DMS replication instance runs in.
Required before creating replication instance

backup_retention_period = 0-35 days (0 = disabled).
Point-in-time recovery to any second within window.
Stored in S3 (invisible to you). backup_window sets when they run (UTC)

Automated: retained for backup_retention_period days, then deleted.
Manual: retained until you delete them. final_snapshot_identifier creates a manual snapshot on destroy

60-120 seconds — RDS promotes standby to primary, updates DNS endpoint.
Application must handle reconnection.
Connection pooling (RDS Proxy) reduces reconnection impact

Managed connection pooler for RDS — reduces connection overhead, handles failover transparently, integrates with Secrets Manager and IAM auth.
Useful for Lambda and ECS with many short-lived connections

cards.front: What is RDS Aurora vs RDS PostgreSQL?
cards.back:
Aurora: AWS-optimized, distributed storage auto-grows, up to 15 replicas, failover <30 sec, ~20% more expensive.
RDS PostgreSQL: standard engine, EBS storage, 5 replicas, failover 60-120 sec, lower cost

DatabaseConnections — pool exhaustion
FreeStorageSpace — disk full = outage
ReadLatency / WriteLatency — query performance
ReplicaLag — replica falling behind
CPUUtilization
FreeableMemory
BurstBalance — t3 credit exhaustion

cards.front: What is MongoDB Atlas on AWS?
cards.back:
MongoDB's managed cloud service.
Deploys into Atlas-managed VPCs, connected to your VPC via VPC peering or AWS PrivateLink.
PrivateLink preferred at banks — traffic stays on AWS backbone.
Terraform Atlas provider

Unified analytics platform on Apache Spark.
Control plane managed by Databricks, data plane runs in your VPC.
Connects to S3 for data lake, RDS for structured data.
IAM roles for auth.
Terraform Databricks provider

AWS-native NoSQL key-value and document database.
Serverless, auto-scales, single-digit millisecond latency.
No schema.
Used for session state, IoT, gaming.
Not RDS — separate service

Managed Elasticsearch/OpenSearch service.
Used for log analytics, full-text search, SIEM data.
Deployed in VPC.
Used at VA.gov for search tier and at Mosaic451 for threat analytics

cards.front: RDS vs DynamoDB — when to use which?
cards.back:
RDS: structured data, complex queries, joins, ACID transactions, existing SQL workloads.
DynamoDB: simple key-value lookups, massive scale, serverless, flexible schema, single-digit ms latency

Managed in-memory cache — Redis or Memcached.
Used to reduce RDS load by caching frequent queries.
Redis supports persistence, pub/sub, sorted sets.
Deployed in VPC, same subnet group pattern as RDS

cards.front: How do EKS pods connect to RDS?
cards.back:
Three options: 1.
Secrets Manager (fetch at startup).
2.
IAM auth via IRSA (token, no password).
3.
RDS Proxy (connection pooling + IAM).
IRSA is the most secure — no credentials stored anywhere

cards.front: How to containerize a COTS tool?
cards.back:
1.
Vendor base image or minimal OS.
2.
Install COTS in Dockerfile.
3.
Externalize config via env vars or ConfigMaps.
4.
Externalize state to PV, RDS, or S3.
5.
Health check endpoint.
6.
Run as non-root

cards.front: What is rehydration in AWS context?
cards.back:
Rebuilding infrastructure from scratch in a new region or account using IaC.
Terraform code IS the recovery plan — point backend to new region, run apply.
GitLab CI/CD automates one-click rehydration

cards.front: ECS execution role vs task role?
cards.back:
Execution role: ECS agent uses it to pull ECR images and write CloudWatch logs.
Task role: running container assumes it for app-level access (RDS IAM auth, S3, Secrets Manager).
Always split them — least privilege

cards.front: How to pass RDS credentials to ECS task?
cards.back:
Use secrets block in task definition: { name = "DB_PASSWORD", valueFrom = "<secrets-manager-arn>" }.
ECS agent fetches at task start.
Never use environment block for secrets — visible in console and logs

local.name is CORRECT — singular. locals.name will error: "An argument named locals is not expected here".
This is the most common mistake under interview pressure

Same-region: replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.main.identifier (just the name).
Cross-region: replicate_source_db = aws_db_instance.main.arn (full ARN).
Using identifier for cross-region will error

Plan error: "final_snapshot_identifier is required when skip_final_snapshot is false".
Always pair: skip_final_snapshot = false + final_snapshot_identifier = "mydb-final-snapshot"

cards.front: Subnet group with 1 AZ + multi_az = true?
cards.back:
Apply error — Multi-AZ requires subnets in at least 2 different AZs in the subnet group.
Always pass 2+ subnet IDs from different AZs in var.subnet_ids

Principal Service must be dms.amazonaws.com — NOT ec2.amazonaws.com.
Common mistake: copy-pasting EC2 role template.
DMS cannot assume a role with wrong principal

family = "postgres16" must match engine_version = "16.x".
Using postgres15 family with engine 16.3 causes apply error.
Check family naming: postgres16, mysql8.0, oracle-ee-19

AWS rejects the configuration — windows must not overlap.
backup_window = "03:00-04:00"
maintenance_window = "sun:04:00-sun:05:00"
Back-to-back is correct, not overlapping

Free tier only supports gp2 — gp3 incurs charges.
For practice: storage_type = "gp2".
For interview answers: storage_type = "gp3".
Remember to switch when writing production code

cards.front: First thing to do in CoderPad?
cards.back:
Read the prompt fully before typing.
Identify: existing VPC or new? Which engine? What extensions (replica, multi-AZ, DMS)? Outline in comments first — shows structured thinking

terraform fmt → terraform init -upgrade → terraform validate → terraform plan -var="..." → terraform apply.
Always -upgrade on init — CoderPad caches stale providers. fmt first shows discipline

CoderPad caches provider plugins between sessions.
Stale cache causes init to fail or use wrong provider version. -upgrade forces download of latest allowed version.
Learned this the hard way

cards.front: How to verify locals without apply in CoderPad?
cards.back:
Add output block with all local values, run terraform plan -var="environment=prod".
Outputs appear in plan output — interviewer can verify conditional logic without waiting for apply

cards.front: What to say while typing in CoderPad?
cards.back:
Explain WHY not just WHAT.
'I'm using manage_master_user_password because passwords should never appear in tfstate — at a bank this is non-negotiable.'
Silence = uncertainty in interviewer's mind

cards.front: Interviewer shares a code fragment — what to do?
cards.back:
Read it fully before touching it.
Identify what's missing.
Add locals first if not present, then data sources, then resources, then outputs.
Don't delete their code — extend it

storage_encrypted=true (at-rest, mandatory at banks). manage_master_user_password (Secrets Manager, never tfstate). publicly_accessible=false (never on internet). deletion_protection=true (no accidental destroy).
Say these out loud

cards.front: What if you forget a required argument?
cards.back:
terraform validate will catch it immediately — run it before plan.
Common forgotten args: final_snapshot_identifier (when skip_final_snapshot=false), subnet_ids (db_subnet_group), family (parameter_group)

cards.front: How to handle 'extend this to multi-region'?
cards.back:
Add provider alias block first: provider "aws" { alias="dr"; region="us-west-2" }.
Then add cross-region replica resource with provider=aws.dr and replicate_source_db = main ARN (not identifier)

cards.front: What to say when done coding?
cards.back:
'I'd also add in production:
- ALB in front
- CloudWatch alarms for FreeStorageSpace and ReplicaLag
- RDS Proxy for connection pooling
- Tighten SG from VPC CIDR to app tier SG only'
Shows production thinking

cards.front: How to handle a locals block the interviewer wrote?
cards.back:
Reference their locals exactly as written — local.their_variable_name.
Add your own locals in the same block or a second locals block.
Never redefine a local they already defined — Terraform errors on duplicate

cards.front: What if CoderPad has no AWS credentials?
cards.back:
terraform plan still works — it validates config and shows what would be created. terraform apply will fail on auth.
Focus on plan output — that's what the interviewer is evaluating, not actual AWS resources

0-5 min: read prompt, outline in comments.
5-25 min: write core resource (RDS + subnet group + SG).
25-40 min: add locals, outputs, backend.
40-50 min: validate + plan.
50-60 min: discuss production improvements

cards.front: What if you blank on a resource argument name?
cards.back:
Say 'I'd look up the exact argument name in the Terraform docs — the pattern is clear.' Then write a comment: # manage_master_user_password or similar.
Interviewers care about patterns, not memorized argument names

cards.front: How to show enterprise thinking in CoderPad?
cards.back:
Start with S3 backend (not local state).
Use variables for vpc_id and subnet_ids (not hardcoded).
Use locals for environment-specific values.
Add common_tags to every resource.
These 4 things signal senior engineer
